original_reasoning: Arabic side/refuge and Greek to another place both share directional displacement.
Arabic khanaza = obscene/foul speech, also fahish. Greek chodanos = butt, buttocks. The obscenity-vulgarity field shared. Strong direct domain link.
Arabic 'calamities' aligns plausibly with Greek 'dissolving/destructive' by a shared undoing sense.
Arabic gloss includes the wall/bank of a valley, a bounded gathering edge or enclosure; Greek means a hall or place of talk. Place/enclosure is a plausible shared core.
Arabic farsh/firāsh = 'bedding, cloak-spread'; Greek pharos = 'cloak, large piece of cloth'. Plausible semantic parallel via cloth/spread.

السقب = young camel calf (offspring), also legs of camels (أرجل الإبل), also المسقب = mother who produces male calves. βλώσκω = to come, to go. The birth/emergence of offspring and the concept of 'coming forth' share a genuine conceptual core — the newborn 'comes' into existence. Also the legs (أرجل) connect to locomotion (going).

الثيه = مأوى الغنم (shelter/hiding place for sheep). λήθω = to be hidden, to escape notice, to be concealed. A shelter for animals is a place of concealment/hiding. The core concept of 'hidden from view in an enclosure' is directly shared between a sheep pen and the semantic field of λήθω.
Arabic means grimacing or scowling; Greek means slanting or crosswise, a plausible drift through crooked facial expression.
